title: MM-curves
speaker: Mario Kummer
abstract:
For an embedded stable curve over the real numbers we introduce a hyperplane arrangement in the tangent space of the Hilbert scheme. The connected components of its complement are labeled by embeddings of the graph of the stable curve to a compact surface. We explain how the real type of a deformation of the curve is determined by the topology of such a surface. Finally, we prove that a so-called graph curve is the special fiber of a maximal Mumford curve (MM-curve) if and only if the graph is planar.
This is based on a joint work with Bernd Sturmfels and Raluca Vlad.

proof_stages.label: real closed and semi-algebraic setting
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes real closed fields as the base context and recalls semi-algebraic connectedness so that topology of real points remains well behaved over non-Archimedean fields.

proof_stages.label: topological types of smooth real curves
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth projective real curve the relevant discrete data are genus g, the number r of real connected components, and the dividing invariant a, subject to the Klein-Harnack restrictions; M-curves are those with maximal r=g+1.

proof_stages.label: stable Mumford degeneration setup
proof_stages.summary: Over the valuation ring of algebraic Puiseux series, a family with smooth general fiber and stable nodal special fiber has a dual graph; the Mumford/MM hypotheses require rational components, real non-isolated nodes, and maximality conditions.

proof_stages.label: abstract signed ribbon graph and surface
proof_stages.summary: The dual graph is enhanced to a signed ribbon graph by cyclic orderings at vertices and signs on edges, and good cycles are used to attach discs and obtain a compact surface containing the graph.

proof_stages.label: ribbon graph extracted from a real family
proof_stages.summary: The cyclic ordering comes from orienting the real circles of the rational components of the special fiber, while the sign of an edge records the local real smoothing type of the corresponding node.

proof_stages.label: Euler characteristic and planarity obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The associated surface satisfies χ(Σ)=r+1−g and is orientable exactly when a=0, so a maximal Mumford degeneration has χ=2 and therefore forces the dual graph to be planar.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert-scheme hyperplane arrangement and sufficiency
proof_stages.summary: For a projectively embedded singular curve, linear forms on the Hilbert-scheme tangent space determine the smoothing signs, and in the trivalent canonically embedded smooth-Hilbert-point case they are independent, so every sign assignment is realizable and the combinatorial restrictions are sufficient.

proof_stages.label: metric and moduli outlook
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion points to open questions about realizing metric signed ribbon graphs, finding more interesting hyperplane arrangements, and understanding the locus of M-curves in the real Deligne-Mumford moduli space.

examples.name: real Puiseux series as real closed fields
examples.note: Real Puiseux series are mentioned to make the abstract notion of a real closed field concrete before the talk fixes its non-Archimedean real-closed setting.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses real Puiseux series to check the real-closed-field example, citing the algebraic closedness of complex Puiseux series and describing the ordering by the first nonzero coefficient

examples.name: signed tree with all positive edges
examples.note: An audience-proposed tree with all edge signs positive exposes a low-valence/pathological case in the good-cycle construction and prompts the valence-at-least-three clarification.
examples.engagement_reason: the all-positive signed tree is raised only as a quick test case for the ribbon-graph construction, with no clear computation or construction carried out on it

passes.reason: An acyclic sign-trivial graph is raised as an edge/minimal case where the proposed cycle-based construction needs extra hypotheses.

examples.name: configuration of n lines in the plane
